Fulbright University Applied Math · CS Minor
01 — About

Who I am

I'm a student at Fulbright University Vietnam, majoring in Applied Mathematics with a minor in Computer Science — on a full President's Scholarship.

My projects span statistical analysis, machine learning, AI product design, financial optimization, and mathematical theory. I'm drawn to problems where rigorous quantitative thinking meets real human needs.

Outside the classroom, I lead MindFulbright (a campus mental health club with 200+ participants per event), teach English to monks and nuns, and have organized community initiatives reaching 3,000+ people. I believe the best technologists are also deeply human.

Skills & Tools
Python R Statistical Modeling Linear Algebra Optimization (SLSQP) Machine Learning Monte Carlo Data Visualization Google Cloud Flutter Financial Modeling LaTeX Human-Centered Design UI/UX

04 — Work

All Projects

2024 — 2025
Intro to Statistics01
Doctor Stress Level Analysis

Statistical study on 100+ doctors examining how sleep, gender, and career stage affect stress levels.

Hypothesis TestingANOVAPearson rR
What it is
A statistical study on 100+ doctors examining how sleep duration, gender, and career stage affect stress levels — conducted as a group final project for Intro to Statistics.
What I did
  • Built visualizations (histograms, box plots, scatter plots) to explore distributions
  • Ran one-sample t-test comparing doctors' sleep against the recommended 7 hours
  • Conducted two-sample hypothesis testing: male vs. female stress levels
  • Computed correlation (r = −0.566) between sleep and stress
What I learned
Sleep explains 32% of stress variance — significant but not the whole story. I learned that choosing the right statistical test matters as much as running it, and that no single variable tells the full picture.
Linear Algebra02
Book Recommendation via Cosine Similarity

Item-item collaborative filtering system using matrix operations — deployed as a live web app for personalized book recommendations.

Collaborative FilteringMatrix MathPythonWeb App
What it is
A working book recommendation engine using item-item collaborative filtering — deployed as a live website where users enter a title and receive personalized suggestions.
What I did
  • Built a user-item rating matrix from Kaggle data, handling sparse data with NaN imputation
  • Applied mean-centering normalization to remove rating bias across users
  • Computed cosine similarity between book vectors to find nearest neighbors
  • Filtered to users with 10+ ratings and books with 4+ ratings for data quality
What I learned
Cosine similarity is literally just the dot product of normalized vectors — seeing a concept from Linear Algebra class power a real product was a turning point in how I think about math.
Probability03
Stock Price Jump-Diffusion Model

Probabilistic model separating normal stock movements from sudden jumps — with 200 Monte Carlo simulations and an interactive Streamlit app.

Monte CarloStochastic ModelPythonStreamlit
What it is
A discrete-time Jump-Diffusion model that separates "normal" stock drift from sudden shocks (news, crises) — using 5,000 Monte Carlo simulations to forecast price distributions and investment probabilities.
What I did
  • Computed daily log-returns and detected jump days using a 3-sigma threshold
  • Classified jumps as "crisis" or "regular" using Modified Z-scores and MAD
  • Estimated diffusion parameters (μ, σ²) separately from non-jump returns
  • Built a Monte Carlo engine generating 200 price paths with confidence intervals
  • Deployed as an interactive Streamlit app for any stock ticker
What I learned
Real markets aren't Gaussian. Modeling tail events — not just the average — is what separates useful risk models from dangerously naive ones. This project changed how I read financial news.
SIN STOCKS
The Cost of Virtue
Quantitative Finance04
Sin Stock Portfolio Optimization

Quantified the "cost of virtue" in ESG investing using quadratic programming and Modern Portfolio Theory — with backtesting over 14 rolling quarters.

MPTSLSQPBacktestingscipy
What it is
A quantitative finance study asking: how much extra risk do ethical investors actually bear by excluding tobacco, alcohol, and gambling stocks? We framed this as "the cost of virtue."
What I did
  • Formulated portfolio optimization as Quadratic Programming (minimize wᵀΣw)
  • Implemented SLSQP solver with 4 constraint types: budget, return target, no short-selling, sin cap
  • Ran scenario analysis across sin caps of 0%, 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 100%
  • Built rolling-window backtesting: 3-year train, 1-quarter test, 14 periods total
  • Constructed efficient frontiers for Sin-only, Non-Sin, and Full portfolios
What I learned
The optimal sin allocation was ~23% — beyond that, variance stops decreasing. Ethics has a measurable price (~8.6% more risk at 0% sin cap), but it's not as catastrophic as some assume. Numbers can inform values.
💊
Smart Medicine Box
Design & Systems Thinking05
Smart Medicine Box

Human-centered design project building a smart medication reminder for elderly users — from empathy maps to working prototype.

UX ResearchPrototypingSystems Thinking
What it is
A human-centered design project to solve medication non-adherence among elderly users — going from user research all the way to a physical-digital prototype and final pitch.
What I did
  • Conducted user research and built empathy maps to identify key pain points
  • Defined the problem space using systems thinking frameworks
  • Ideated and prototyped a smart box with reminder alerts and compartment tracking
  • Pitched the solution with a full design rationale to a panel of judges
What I learned
The hardest part of design isn't the prototype — it's resisting the urge to jump to solutions before truly understanding the problem. Empathy first, technology second.

Calculus I06
Taylor Series: From Zeno to Rocket Science

Mathematical essay proving Taylor Series from first principles — through Euler's Identity, convergence analysis, and Falcon 9 stabilization.

Mathematical ProofAnalysisLaTeX
What it is
A research essay tracing Taylor Series from Zeno's paradoxes and the Kerala School of Mathematics to modern engineering — with original proofs and real-world applications from calculators to aerospace.
What I did
  • Derived the Taylor Series from scratch using the method of matching derivatives
  • Proved the Lagrange Error Bound via Rolle's Theorem and auxiliary function construction
  • Applied the Ratio Test to determine radius of convergence for ln(1+x)
  • Proved Euler's Identity (e^iπ + 1 = 0) purely from series expansions
  • Showed how sin(θ) ≈ θ linearization enables Falcon 9 stabilization at 1000Hz
What I learned
Madhava in 14th-century India derived trigonometric series 200 years before European mathematicians — yet history barely mentions him. Math is not discovered in a straight line, and whose work gets credited matters.
GDGOC Hackathon 202507
Thân Thanh — AI Alzheimer Companion

AI audiobook platform cloning a family member's voice to support Alzheimer's patients — Top 12 out of 100+ teams across Southern Vietnam.

Voice CloningHealthcare AIFlutterResponsible AI
What it is
An AI platform addressing the gap between physical Alzheimer's care and emotional support — cloning a loved one's voice to read audiobooks, improving memory recall and reducing patient anxiety.
What I did
  • Researched non-pharmacological Alzheimer's interventions to define the problem space
  • Designed the full product: voice pipeline, book library, user flow
  • Built responsible AI framework: consent agreements, access controls, psychological risk mitigation
  • Developed a simulated website prototype demonstrating the core user journey
What I learned
Responsible AI isn't a checklist — it's a design constraint from day one. Voice cloning of deceased relatives can cause real psychological harm if misused. We had to think through ethics before writing a single line of code.
